threats.title: Genesis Depositors Steal Accumulated Gauge Rewards via Missing userRewardPerTokenPaid Checkpoint
threats.hypothesis_description: depositsForGenesis calls updateReward(address(0)) which updates global rewardPerTokenStored but never sets userRewardPerTokenPaid for the actual depositor (_tokenOwner). When the depositor later interacts with the gauge (getReward, withdraw), earned() computes _balanceOf(account) * (rewardPerToken() - userRewardPerTokenPaid[account]) / 1e18, where userRewardPerTokenPaid is 0. This grants them retroactive rewards for the entire period before their deposit, stealing from existing stakers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The updateReward modifier normally receives the user address to checkpoint their reward state. Using address(0) only updates global state. Since genesis balances are tracked via genesisPool.balanceOf() and included in _balanceOf(), the depositor gets a non-zero balance without any reward debt, creating a free-money condition exploitable by the genesis pool contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateReward(address(0)) instead of updateReward(_tokenOwner); _totalSupply increased but _balances[_tokenOwner] not set; balance tracked via external genesisPool.balanceOf()

preconditions: genesisPool is set and calls depositsForGenesis
Gauge has accumulated rewardPerTokenStored > 0 from prior notifyRewardAmount calls
Genesis depositor has userRewardPerTokenPaid == 0 (first interaction)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: userRewardPerTokenPaid[_tokenOwner] remains 0 while rewardPerTokenStored accumulates; _balanceOf includes genesisPool balance giving non-zero effective stake
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No userRewardPerTokenPaid[_tokenOwner] = rewardPerTokenStored assignment after deposit

analysis_questions: Can notifyRewardAmount be called before depositsForGenesis, creating accumulated rewardPerTokenStored?
Does genesisPool.balanceOf() immediately reflect the deposit, making _balanceOf non-zero?
What is the maximum reward theft given typical gauge parameters?
validation_requirements: Confirm updateReward(address(0)) does not checkpoint _tokenOwner
Verify earned() uses _balanceOf which includes genesis balance
Calculate maximum extractable reward: genesisAmount * rewardPerTokenStored / 1e18

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: GenesisPool deposits 100,000 LP for user at time when rewardPerTokenStored = 1e17. User immediately calls getReward() and receives 100000 * 1e17 / 1e18 = 10,000 reward tokens stolen from other stakers.
prerequisites: Active reward distribution before genesis deposit

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of accumulated gauge rewards proportional to genesis deposit size
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All existing gauge stakers lose their share of rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No on-chain recovery; rewards already transferred

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for genesis deposit after reward distribution has started

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Only needs genesis pool deposit capability
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via on-chain reward claim events

threats.title: RouterV2.swapExactTokensForTokensSimple Sends Tokens to address(0) Due to Uninitialized Route Fields
threats.hypothesis_description: swapExactTokensForTokensSimple creates a route struct in memory but never initializes the `pair` or `receiver` fields, leaving them as address(0). getAmountsOut uses routes[i].pair to compute amounts: for non-concentrated, isPair(address(0)) returns false so amounts[1] stays 0 causing revert on amountOutMin check. For concentrated, pairToDeployer(address(0)) returns address(0) causing quoter failure. Even if bypassed, _swap sends output to routes[0].receiver = address(0), permanently losing tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a core user-facing swap function that is completely non-functional. Any user calling this function will waste gas on a guaranteed revert. If amountOutMin is 0 and concentrated=true finds a path, tokens would be sent to address(0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: route[] memory routes = new route[](1); sets from/to/stable/concentrated but never sets routes[0].pair or routes[0].receiver; defaults to address(0)

preconditions: User calls swapExactTokensForTokensSimple with any parameters
No special configuration needed - always fails

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Uninitialized struct fields in memory default to zero values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: routes[0].pair = address(0) breaks getAmountsOut; routes[0].receiver = address(0) would send tokens to burn address
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No assignment of pair = pairFor(...) or receiver = to

analysis_questions: Is this function used by any frontend or integration?
Can concentrated=true path bypass the revert and actually send tokens to address(0)?
Is there any way getAmountsOut succeeds with pair=address(0)?
validation_requirements: Confirm isPair(address(0)) returns false
Confirm pairToDeployer(address(0)) returns address(0)
Test both concentrated and non-concentrated paths

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User calls swapExactTokensForTokensSimple to swap 1000 USDC for WAVAX. Function reverts on getAmountsOut, wasting gas. If somehow bypassed, tokens go to address(0).
prerequisites: Any user interaction with this function

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gas loss on every call; potential permanent token loss if revert is bypassed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting to use this swap function
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Gas fees unrecoverable; tokens at address(0) unrecoverable

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable as consistent revert from this function

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Swap Router Uses Wrong Pool for Amount Calculation Causing Reverts or Suboptimal Swaps
threats.hypothesis_description: _swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens calculates amountOutput via the router's getAmountOut(amountInput, input, output) which returns the BEST amount across both stable AND volatile pools. However, the actual swap is executed on pairFor(routes[i].from, routes[i].to, routes[i].stable) which uses the route's specific stable flag. When the best pool type differs from the route's pool type, the calculated output exceeds what the target pool can produce, causing K invariant failure and transaction revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a systematic failure mode for fee-on-transfer token swaps whenever both stable and volatile pools exist for a token pair and they have different optimal outputs. The function calculates output from pool A but tries to execute the swap on pool B.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getAmountOut(amountInput, input, output) selects best pool type; swap executed on pairFor(..., routes[i].stable) using route-specified pool type

preconditions: Both stable and volatile pools exist for the token pair
The pool with higher output differs from routes[i].stable
User uses fee-on-transfer swap path

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: getAmountOut returns best across pools; swap executed on route-specified pool
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: amountOutput from volatile pool applied to stable pool swap, causing K invariant violation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use pair-specific getAmountOut matching the route's pair

analysis_questions: How many token pairs have both stable and volatile pools deployed?
What is the typical output difference between stable and volatile pools?
Does this affect all FoT swap paths or only specific configurations?
validation_requirements: Test with token pair having both pool types with different optimal outputs
Verify K invariant failure when using mismatched amounts
Confirm getAmountOut selects cross-pool best amount

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User routes FoT token swap through stable pool. Volatile pool gives higher output. getAmountOut returns volatile amount. Swap on stable pool with inflated output amount fails K check, reverting. All FoT swaps on dual-pool pairs become unreliable.
prerequisites: Both stable and volatile pools exist for token pair

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gas loss on failed transactions; inability to swap FoT tokens through affected pairs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users swapping FoT tokens through dual-pool pairs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No fund loss, but functionality broken

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for FoT swaps with both stable and volatile pools

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable as consistent swap reverts on dual-pool pairs

threats.title: TradeHelper._pairFor Computes Incorrect CREATE2 Addresses Breaking GlobalRouter Swaps
threats.hypothesis_description: TradeHelper._pairFor computes CREATE2 addresses using factory (PairFactory) as the deployer, but pairs are actually deployed by PairGenerator via CREATE2. Additionally, pairCodeHash is keccak256(type(Pair).creationCode) which excludes constructor arguments, while CREATE2 init code includes ABI-encoded constructor args. Both the deployer address and init code hash are wrong, making all computed addresses incorrect. GlobalRouter._swap routes swaps through these wrong addresses.
threats.investigation_rationale: PairGenerator.createPair uses `new Pair{salt: salt}(factory, token0, token1, stable)` - the deployer is PairGenerator (not PairFactory), and the init code hash must include constructor args. TradeHelper uses PairFactory as deployer and creation code without args, producing completely different addresses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: CREATE2 address computed with factory as deployer and keccak256(type(Pair).creationCode) as init code hash; actual deployment uses PairGenerator and includes constructor args in init code

preconditions: GlobalRouter or any integrator uses TradeHelper.pairFor for routing
Actual pairs deployed via PairFactory -> PairGenerator pipeline

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Wrong deployer address (factory vs PairGenerator) and wrong init code hash (missing constructor args)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: All computed pair addresses are incorrect; swaps route to non-existent contracts
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use PairFactory.getPair() lookup instead of CREATE2 computation

analysis_questions: Does GlobalRouter actually use TradeHelper.pairFor for swap routing?
Are there any other integrators using TradeHelper.pairFor?
Could an attacker deploy contracts at the predicted (wrong) addresses?
validation_requirements: Compute TradeHelper._pairFor output vs PairFactory.getPair output for existing pairs
Verify PairGenerator is the actual CREATE2 deployer
Confirm init code hash mismatch due to constructor args

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User swaps via GlobalRouter. TradeHelper computes wrong pair address. _swap calls swap() on non-existent contract, reverting. If attacker deploys malicious contract at the predicted address, tokens could be stolen.
prerequisites: Any swap through GlobalRouter using TradeHelper routing

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: GlobalRouter completely non-functional; potential token theft if malicious contracts deployed at predicted addresses
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on whether tokens were sent to wrong address vs revert

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing TradeHelper.pairFor output vs actual pair addresses

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Could deploy contract at predicted address for token theft
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately observable as GlobalRouter swap failures

threats.title: RouterV2._swap Ignores _to Parameter Using routes[i].receiver Instead, Breaking swapExactTokensForETH
threats.hypothesis_description: The _swap function completely ignores its _to parameter and uses routes[i].receiver for directing swap outputs. For swapExactTokensForETH, _swap is called with address(this) as _to, but the last route's receiver must be address(this) for WETH to arrive at the router for unwrapping. Since routes are user-provided calldata, if the user doesn't correctly set routes[last].receiver = router_address, the WETH goes elsewhere and wETH.withdraw fails, or the _to contract never receives the unwrapped ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _to parameter in _swap appears in the function signature but is never referenced in the function body. All output routing uses routes[i].receiver from user calldata. This creates a mismatch between the documented API and actual behavior.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _to parameter in _swap signature is never used; routes[i].receiver controls all token destinations; swapExactTokensForETH expects _to=address(this) but doesn't enforce it in routes

preconditions: User calls swapExactTokensForTokens or swapExactTokensForETH
Routes calldata doesn't have last route receiver matching expected destination

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dead _to parameter; all routing via routes[i].receiver
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: swapExactTokensForETH calls _swap(amounts, routes, address(this)) but routes[last].receiver may not be address(this)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No assertion that routes[last].receiver matches _to; _to is dead code

analysis_questions: How does the frontend construct routes - does it set receiver correctly?
Can routes from BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut be used directly with swapExactTokensForETH?
Is there any validation that routes[last].receiver == expected destination?
validation_requirements: Confirm _to parameter is never referenced in _swap body
Test swapExactTokensForETH with routes[last].receiver != address(this)
Verify WETH withdrawal failure path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User calls swapExactTokensForETH with routes constructed from API where receiver is set to user address. WETH goes to user instead of router. Router tries wETH.withdraw on 0 balance, reverts. User gets WETH instead of ETH (inconvenience) or loses funds if receiver was set incorrectly.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Failed ETH swaps; potential fund misdirection
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: WETH is recoverable if sent to user; lost if sent to address(0)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying _to parameter usage in _swap

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable as swap revert or incorrect token destination

threats.title: TradeHelper.getAmountsIn Infinite Loop Due to uint Underflow in Decrementing Loop Counter
threats.hypothesis_description: getAmountsIn uses `for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--)` where i is uint256. When i reaches 0, the decrement i-- causes underflow to type(uint256).max (Solidity 0.8.13 unchecked in for-loop post-condition). The condition `i >= 0` is always true for unsigned integers, creating an infinite loop that exhausts all gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a well-known pattern bug with unsigned integer decrementing loops. The function is completely non-functional for any input, rendering reverse-path amount calculations impossible for all integrators.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--) -- uint underflow when i=0, i-- wraps to max; i >= 0 always true

preconditions: Any call to getAmountsIn with valid routes

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Loop never terminates; always consumes all gas
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use explicit break condition or different loop structure

analysis_questions: Is getAmountsIn called by any on-chain contract in the protocol?
Are there off-chain integrators relying on this function?
Does this block any critical protocol operation?
validation_requirements: Call getAmountsIn with any valid route and confirm out-of-gas revert
Verify uint decrement behavior at i=0 in Solidity 0.8.13

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any call to getAmountsIn always reverts with out-of-gas. Function is completely non-functional.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gas loss on every call; broken reverse routing for integrators

threats.title: TradeHelper.getAmountInStable Uses Inverted Formula (Addition Instead of Subtraction) Producing Wrong Quotes
threats.hypothesis_description: getAmountInStable calculates y_1 = reserveOut + output (line adds output to reserve) instead of the correct y_1 = reserveOut - output. When computing the required input for a desired output, the target state should have LESS of the output token in the pool, not MORE. Additionally, the final conversion uses decimalsOut instead of decimalsIn for scaling the result. Both errors produce completely wrong amountIn values.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Newton-Raphson iteration converges on the wrong target point since y_1 is too high. The function calculates the input needed to ADD more output tokens to the pool rather than remove them, giving fundamentally incorrect results.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint y_1 = reserveOut + output; should be reserveOut - output. Also line ~102: amountInNoFees = ((reserveIn - x_1) * decimalsOut / 1e18) uses decimalsOut instead of decimalsIn

preconditions: Any call to getAmountInStable for a stable pool

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: y_1 = reserveOut + output (wrong direction); decimalsOut used instead of decimalsIn
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Returns completely incorrect amountIn values for stable pool reverse quotes
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No sanity check on output (e.g., amountIn should increase with amountOut)

analysis_questions: Is getAmountInStable used by any on-chain swap execution path?
Do integrators use this for slippage calculations?
Can incorrect quotes lead to user fund loss in any integration?
validation_requirements: Compare getAmountInStable output vs correct calculation
Test with known reserve values to quantify error magnitude
Verify decimals scaling error impact

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Integrator uses getAmountInStable to determine how many tokens to provide for a desired output. Receives incorrect (likely much too small) value. Swap fails or user gets less output than expected.
prerequisites: Any stable pool reverse quote via TradeHelper

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect quotes could lead to failed trades or suboptimal execution by integrators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Integrators and protocols relying on TradeHelper for pricing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No direct fund loss from view function; indirect loss via bad trade execution

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing getAmountInStable output vs correct mathematical result

threats.title: GaugeExtraRewarder Uses TOKEN.balanceOf(GAUGE) Instead of _totalSupply Creating Reward Distribution Mismatch
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeExtraRewarder.updatePool and _pendingReward use IERC20(IGauge(GAUGE).TOKEN()).balanceOf(GAUGE) as lpSupply for reward calculations. However, GaugeV2 uses its internal _totalSupply for main reward calculations. If someone sends LP tokens directly to the gauge without calling deposit(), TOKEN.balanceOf(GAUGE) > _totalSupply. This dilutes extra rewards for legitimate stakers while main rewards remain unaffected, creating an asymmetry between reward streams that can be exploited.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can donate LP tokens to the gauge to inflate balanceOf(GAUGE), diluting extra rewards per share without affecting their main gauge rewards. The cost is the donated LP tokens; the gain is reduced extra reward distribution to other stakers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 lpSupply = IERC20(IGauge(GAUGE).TOKEN()).balanceOf(GAUGE); -- uses actual balance instead of gauge._totalSupply

preconditions: GaugeExtraRewarder is set as gaugeRewarder for a gauge
Attacker has LP tokens to donate
Extra rewards are actively being distributed

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: balanceOf(GAUGE) used instead of gauge._totalSupply for reward calculation denominator
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Donating tokens to gauge inflates denominator, diluting rewards for all stakers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use gauge.totalSupply() instead of TOKEN.balanceOf(GAUGE)

analysis_questions: How large is the typical extra reward compared to main rewards?
What is the cost/benefit of LP token donation attack?
Can the donated tokens be recovered via skim or sync?
validation_requirements: Compare balanceOf(GAUGE) vs gauge._totalSupply after donation
Calculate reward dilution percentage per donated LP
Test reward claim after donation to verify reduced amounts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker sends 1000 LP tokens directly to gauge (without deposit). balanceOf(GAUGE) increases by 1000 but _totalSupply stays the same. Extra rewards are now divided by a larger denominator, reducing rewards per share for all stakers. Attacker's main gauge rewards are unaffected since they use _totalSupply.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Reduced extra rewards for all legitimate stakers proportional to donation size
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All gauge stakers receiving extra rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Donated LP tokens stuck in gauge; effect persists until tokens removed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing balanceOf vs _totalSupply impact on extra rewards

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing TOKEN.balanceOf(GAUGE) vs gauge.totalSupply()

threats.title: Unrestricted Pair.claimStakingFees() enables anyone to siphon staking-fee bucket to a potentially unset/incorrect handler (fund loss/DoS)
threats.hypothesis_description: Any address can call Pair.claimStakingFees(), which forwards all accumulated staking fees from PairFees to PairFactory.stakingFeeHandler(). If stakingFeeHandler is unset (zero) or misconfigured, an unprivileged caller can force funds to be sent to the wrong recipient (including address(0)) or repeatedly grief the intended operational timing of fee withdrawals.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an external entry point that moves value out of PairFees without any authorization. Even if the destination is intended, public triggering can create economic/operational abuse and permanent loss if handler is mis-set. Validate whether the protocol intended permissionless harvesting and whether stakingFeeHandler is always set before fees accumulate.
deployment_assumptions: PairFactory.stakingFeeHandler is unset (address(0)) at any time after pairs begin accruing staking fees, OR can be changed to an incorrect address
Pairs accrue staking fees (stakingNFTFee > 0) so PairFees.toStake0/toStake1 becomes nonzero
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script in provided context shows PairFactory.setStakingFeeAddress being called prior to trading; Pair.claimStakingFees is permissionless and uses stakingFeeHandler() directly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external claimStakingFees() has no access control; reads PairFactory(factory).stakingFeeHandler() then PairFees(fees).withdrawStakingFees(_feehandler)

preconditions: stakingNFTFee configured > 0 so staking fees accrue into PairFees.toStake0/toStake1
stakingFeeHandler is unset, incorrect, or can be reconfigured unexpectedly
Sufficient fees exist in PairFees balances

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: Pair.sol lines 88-92; PairFees.sol withdrawStakingFees() transfers then zeroes buckets
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless value-moving function + destination taken from mutable factory config
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: public caller can trigger transfer to any configured handler; if handler==0, funds burn; if mis-set, funds redirected
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No onlyOwner/onlyFeeManager/onlyStakingHandler gate; no recipient!=0 validation in claimStakingFees()

analysis_questions: Is stakingFeeHandler always set before any pair can accrue staking fees?
Can stakingFeeHandler be changed while toStake balances are nonzero, enabling redirection?
Does PairFactory.setStakingFeeAddress enforce immutability or timelocks elsewhere?

Simulate swaps accruing staking fees then call claimStakingFees with stakingFeeHandler=0
Check governance controls around updating stakingFeeHandler

threats.title: Pair.sample() and Pair.quote() lack bounds checks, enabling attacker-triggered reverts that can break any on-chain flow that depends on them (oracle/route DoS)
threats.hypothesis_description: Pair.sample() computes `i = length - (points * window)` where `length = observations.length - 1`. If `points*window > length`, underflow occurs and the loop accesses observations out of bounds, reverting. Pair.quote() relies on sample() and will also revert. If any state-changing functions in other contracts depend on quote/sample/current for validation (e.g., slippage checks, oracle-based limits), an attacker can trigger or sustain a state where those operations revert by choosing parameters that cause underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: While sample/quote are view, they are public and could be used by other state-changing components (not shown here) or external keepers. The lack of validation is a code defect; if used in critical paths, it becomes a DoS vector. Must validate call graph in the broader system.
deployment_assumptions: A state-changing function in-scope calls Pair.quote/sample/current internally (or depends on them via external call) without sanitizing inputs
Attackers can influence the parameters (points/window/granularity) used
threats.config_evidence: No on-chain call sites in provided contracts for Pair.sample/quote/current besides user/view calls; need Phase D call graph across full repo.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint i = length - (points * window); no require(points*window <= length); potential underflow and OOB array access

preconditions: observations.length is small relative to attacker-chosen points/window
Caller can reach the state-changing path (if any) that relies on these functions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked arithmetic on array indices; no bounds validation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: underflow makes i huge -> observations[nextIndex] revert

analysis_questions: Are Pair.quote/sample/current used by any non-view operations (e.g., liquidation, swap limits, epoch automation) elsewhere in the full protocol?
Do any API/helper contracts call them in transactions (not just eth_call)?
Could external automation be blocked by these reverts?
validation_requirements: Full-repo search for `.sample(` / `.quote(` / `.current(` in non-view functions
Fuzz sample() inputs to map revert conditions
If used, add parameter sanitization tests

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a keeper or contract calls Pair.quote() during a transaction with user-controlled `granularity`, attacker supplies large value so quote() reverts, blocking the transaction and potentially halting protocol operations relying on it.
prerequisites: Dependent on integration using these functions in write paths

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Primarily DoS; can block withdrawals/liquidations if those rely on quotes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users interacting with any dependent operation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High if fixed in code; otherwise requires operational workarounds

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for insufficient observations / bounds behavior

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: single transaction if used in write path
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor revert reasons around oracle/quote usage

threats.title: BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() can be abused to force extremely high gas usage (quadratic scanning) in on-chain calls, potentially breaking integrators that call it in transactions
threats.hypothesis_description: BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() scans all pairs in both the basic factory and Algebra factory, then performs nested loops over (totBasicPairs + totConcentratedPairs)^2 to search 2-hop and 3-hop paths, with additional quoter calls in-loop. If any on-chain integration (or keeper) calls this function in a transaction, it can easily exceed block gas limits and revert. An attacker can further increase cost by ensuring factories have many pairs (permissionless pair creation in PairFactory) so the scan becomes prohibitively expensive.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function is non-view (no view modifier) and uses try/catch external calls inside loops. Even if intended for off-chain use, it is callable on-chain and could be mistakenly integrated. Pair creation is permissionless, so pair count growth is attacker-influenceable over time.
deployment_assumptions: Some production system calls BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() on-chain (tx) rather than via eth_call
PairFactory.createPair remains permissionless allowing an attacker to inflate totBasicPairs; Algebra factory may also grow
threats.config_evidence: BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut is declared `external returns` (not view), signaling it may be used in tx flows; no explicit guards prevent on-chain usage.

inventory_refs.function: getAmountOut(uint amountIn, address tokenIn, address tokenOut, address _userAddress)

proof_of_signal.pattern: multiple full scans of factories + nested i/j loops over pairs; quoter calls inside loops; function is not view

preconditions: Factories have moderate-to-large number of pairs (hundreds+)
A transaction context calls getAmountOut() (not an eth_call)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unbounded iteration over user-independent global state (pair registries)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: gas blowup grows with number of pairs; attacker can bloat pair count permissionlessly
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No max hops/pairs scanned per call; no pagination; no view modifier to discourage tx usage

analysis_questions: Is getAmountOut() ever called by other contracts in a state-changing manner (routers, genesis, auctions)?
What are the expected pair counts on mainnet and growth assumptions?
Should this function be view and/or restricted to off-chain usage patterns?
validation_requirements: Search for on-chain callers of BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut
Benchmark gas for various pair counts on a fork
Assess pair-creation spam feasibility/cost on Avalanche

If any contract calls BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() in a tx (e.g., during a swap route selection), those txs revert due to gas, causing a persistent DoS for that integration.

threats.title: Pair TWAP Oracle calculates ratio of average reserves instead of average price
threats.hypothesis_description: The Pair contract's oracle accumulates raw reserves over time rather than the price ratio, making the resulting TWAP highly susceptible to manipulation via short-term extreme reserve imbalances.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _update, reserve0CumulativeLast += _reserve0 * timeElapsed. The current() function then calculates the TWAP price by dividing the average of reserve1 by the average of reserve0. Mathematically, the ratio of averages is not equal to the average of ratios. An attacker can use a flash loan to create a massive reserve imbalance for a single block, drastically skewing the average reserve and manipulating the TWAP price reported to other protocols.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Accumulates raw reserves instead of UQ112x112 price ratios

preconditions: External protocols rely on Pair.current() or Pair.quote() for TWAP pricing
Flash loans are available for the pool's assets

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: TWAP = Average(Reserve1) / Average(Reserve0)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loaning to inflate one reserve massively increases its average, skewing the final division
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker manipulates oracle to exploit lending markets or synthetic asset minting that relies on this TWAP

threats.title: Pair stable curve _k calculation rounds to zero for small balances
threats.hypothesis_description: The stable pool invariant calculation (_k) divides by 1e18 multiple times, causing it to round down to 0 for small reserve balances, allowing an attacker to drain the remaining tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: In the _k function, _a = (_x * _y) / 1e18. If _x and _y are small (e.g., near the MINIMUM_LIQUIDITY of 1000 wei), _a rounds down to 0, making the entire _k return 0. Because the swap function only requires _k(balance0, balance1) >= _k(reserve0, reserve1), if the initial _k is 0, any resulting balance combination that yields a _k >= 0 is accepted. An attacker can swap out almost all remaining tokens, effectively draining the pool.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Multiple divisions by 1e18 cause precision loss to zero for small values

preconditions: Stable pool exists with small reserve balances (e.g., after massive liquidity removal)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _a = (_x * _y) / 1e18 rounds to 0 if _x * _y < 1e18
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker removes liquidity to push reserves low, then executes a swap that drains the rest
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals the remaining MINIMUM_LIQUIDITY and any other dust in the pool

economic_questions: What is the exact threshold of reserves where _k becomes 0?
Can an attacker profitably force a pool into this state?
validation_requirements: Test stable pool with 1000 wei reserves
Verify _k returns 0 and swap allows draining the pool

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: MINIMUM_LIQUIDITY value (usually negligible, but could be high for high-value low-decimal tokens)

threats.title: RouterV2 multi-hop swaps mixing CL and basic pools leak value or revert
threats.hypothesis_description: When routing a swap through a concentrated liquidity (CL) pool followed by a basic pool, the router uses a pre-calculated predicted output for the basic pool swap, causing reverts or value leakage if the actual CL output differs.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _swap, basic pool hops request the exact predicted output amount (amounts[i+1]) calculated by getAmountsOut prior to execution. If a preceding CL hop returns more tokens than predicted (positive slippage), the excess is left in the basic pool because it only outputs the predicted amount. If the CL hop returns fewer tokens (negative slippage), the basic pool swap reverts due to K invariant failure because it doesn't have enough input to support the hardcoded output.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Basic pool swap uses pre-calculated amounts[i+1] instead of actual balance delta

preconditions: User executes a multi-hop swap where a CL pool precedes a basic pool

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users lose funds to the basic pool (donation) or suffer transaction reverts

threats.title: TradeHelper getAmountInVolatile rounds down causing exact output swaps to revert
threats.hypothesis_description: The getAmountInVolatile function uses integer division without rounding up, causing it to return an input amount slightly less than mathematically required to satisfy the pair's K invariant.
threats.investigation_rationale: The calculation amountIn = (amountOut * reserveIn / (reserveOut - amountOut)) * 10000 / (10000 - fee) truncates the result due to integer division. Because the Pair contract strictly enforces the K invariant, providing a truncated (smaller) input amount will cause the swap to fail the invariant check and revert. This breaks any integration relying on TradeHelper for exact-output routing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Integer division without +1 rounding adjustment for input calculation

preconditions: Integrator or frontend uses getAmountInVolatile to calculate required input for a swap

threats.title: Unbounded Referral Fee Setting Can DoS All Swaps via Arithmetic Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: PairFactory.setReferralFee and setCustomReferralFee have no upper bound validation. If MAX_REFERRAL_FEE or a custom referral fee is set > 10000, then in Pair._update0/_update1, `_referralFee = amount * _maxRef / 10000` exceeds `amount`, causing `amount -= _referralFee` to underflow and revert (Solidity 0.8.13). This permanently DoSes all swaps on affected pairs.
threats.investigation_rationale: setReferralFee has zero validation (unlike setFee which checks <= MAX_FEE). A value of 10001 makes _referralFee > amount, causing underflow revert in _update0/_update1 which is called from every swap.

threats.title: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe Tautological Zero-Address Check Allows Breaking Fee Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe has require(_int >= address(0), 'ZA') which is always true since all addresses are >= address(0) as uint160. threats.title: Permissionless GaugeFactory.createGauge enables attacker-controlled gauges that may be whitelisted downstream, enabling reward/fee capture or DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeFactory.createGauge is permissionless and deploys a new GaugeV2 with attacker-chosen constructor parameters (reward token, LP token, distribution, bribe addresses, genesis manager). If any downstream system (e.g., GaugeManager/Voter/UI) treats GaugeFactory output as implicitly trusted, an attacker can create deceptive gauges for legitimate LP tokens, route users to stake, and then disrupt operations (e.g., set DISTRIBUTION to attacker EOA so notifyRewardAmount never happens, or set bribe addresses to attacker-controlled contracts). Even without direct theft, this can cause operational confusion and loss of rewards/fees for stakers who deposit into the wrong gauge.
threats.investigation_rationale: Permissionless gauge creation is a frequent real-world ops issue when downstream relies on 'factory provenance' instead of explicit whitelisting. Phase A notes createGauge is permissionless regardless of roles.
deployment_assumptions: Downstream components (GaugeManager/Voter/registry/UI) accept or display any GaugeFactory-created gauge without an allowlist
Users can be induced to stake into the attacker-created gauge via on-chain discoverability (not requiring phishing; e.g., auto-indexers)
threats.config_evidence: No in-scope GaugeManager/Voter code provided; must verify whether gauges are explicitly registered/whitelisted elsewhere

proof_of_signal.pattern: function createGauge(...) external returns (address) { last_gauge = address(new GaugeV2(...)); __gauges.push(last_gauge); } // no access control

preconditions: Attacker can pay deployment gas for GaugeV2 creation
At least one downstream path treats GaugeFactory gauges as valid without separate admin registration
Users or automation can interact with the attacker gauge for staking

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Factory provenance may be misinterpreted as authorization; GaugeV2 parameters are entirely attacker-controlled at deploy time
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None to create; downstream trust decision determines impact

threats.title: GaugeV2 notifyRewardAmount Solvency Check Includes Unclaimed Rewards Leading to Late Claimer Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: notifyRewardAmount checks `rewardRate <= balance / DURATION` where `balance = rewardToken.balanceOf(address(this))`. This balance includes previously unclaimed user rewards (still sitting in rewards[] mapping). When a new reward period begins, the solvency check appears to pass because it counts tokens owed to old claimers as available for new distribution. As old claimers withdraw, the balance drops below what the new period needs, causing late claimers in the new period to receive nothing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The solvency invariant (rewardRate * DURATION <= actual available tokens) can be violated because 'available tokens' is conflated with 'total balance including owed amounts'. This is a concrete accounting flaw in the reward distribution mechanism.

threats.title: GaugeV2 Deposit Updates State Before Token Transfer Creating Extra Rewarder Accounting Discrepancy
threats.hypothesis_description: In GaugeV2._deposit, _balances and _totalSupply are updated BEFORE TOKEN.safeTransferFrom, and the gaugeRewarder.onReward callback fires BEFORE the token transfer. GaugeExtraRewarder.updatePool reads TOKEN.balanceOf(GAUGE) as lpSupply, which at this point doesn't include the deposit (tokens haven't transferred yet). The accRewardPerShare is computed with OLD lpSupply, but user.amount is set to the NEW _balanceOf. On withdraw, the reverse occurs: lpSupply still includes withdrawn tokens during onReward. This creates a persistent small accounting drift in accRewardPerShare that compounds over many deposits/withdrawals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ordering of state updates vs token transfers creates a window where GaugeExtraRewarder sees inconsistent state. Each deposit/withdraw introduces a small rounding asymmetry in accRewardPerShare that accumulates.

Extra rewards are actively distributing

threats.title: Pair claimFees Lacks Reentrancy Guard Enabling Cross-Function Reentrancy with Hook Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: Pair.claimFees() has no lock modifier (unlike swap/mint/burn/skim/sync). It calls PairFees.claimFeesFor which transfers token0/token1 to msg.sender. If either token has transfer hooks (ERC777, ERC4626, or custom callbacks), the recipient can re-enter the Pair contract. While claimFees itself follows CEI (sets claimable to 0 before transfer), the attacker can re-enter via claimStakingFees() (also unprotected by lock) to trigger withdrawStakingFees, or re-enter transferFrom to manipulate balanceOf and trigger _updateFor with stale index data.

threats.title: GaugeExtraRewarder setDistributionRate Remaining Reward Accounting Can Overstate Available Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: setDistributionRate calculates notDistributed = rewardPerSecond * timeLeft and adds it to the new amount. It then checks IERC20(rewardToken).balanceOf(address(this)) >= amount. However, between setDistributionRate calls, some rewards may have been transferred out via onReward but not yet reflected in a reduced rewardPerSecond. If updatePool hasn't been called recently, accRewardPerShare may be stale, and the actual tokens available may be less than rewardPerSecond * timeLeft suggests, because some rewards have already been claimed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The notDistributed calculation uses rewardPerSecond * timeLeft which represents scheduled-but-not-yet-distributed rewards. But some of those rewards may have already been distributed (claimed by users via onReward). The balance check catches extreme cases but may not account for the gap between scheduled and actually-remaining rewards.

threats.title: PairFactory.setReferralFee Has No Upper Bound Allowing DoS of All Swaps via Arithmetic Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: setReferralFee and setCustomReferralFee have no upper bound checks on the referral fee value. If set to > 10000, then in Pair._update0/_update1, `_referralFee = amount * _maxRef / 10000` produces a value larger than `amount`, and `amount -= _referralFee` causes an arithmetic underflow revert in Solidity 0.8.13. This permanently DoSes all swaps on all pairs (or specific pairs for customReferralFees) until the fee is corrected.
threats.investigation_rationale: While this requires feeManager privilege, the impact is protocol-wide DoS of all trading. Even a value slightly above 10000 would cause all swap fees to revert, effectively halting the entire DEX.

threats.title: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe Tautological Validation Allows Setting address(0) Breaking Fee Distribution

threats.title: Pair.permit() recomputes DOMAIN_SEPARATOR each call and stores it; potential signature confusion across forks or name changes (non-standard EIP-2612 behavior)
threats.hypothesis_description: Pair.permit() recomputes and overwrites DOMAIN_SEPARATOR using current chainId and `name` each call, rather than using an immutable DOMAIN_SEPARATOR computed at deployment. While it uses block.chainid (good), the stored DOMAIN_SEPARATOR becomes mutable state and diverges from common EIP-2612 patterns. If the Pair name could ever differ across deployments or be altered (not in this code, but depends on construction inputs), or if a chain fork changes chainId assumptions, signatures may be unexpectedly accepted/rejected, potentially enabling replay in edge deployments or breaking integrator assumptions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Not an obvious direct theft vector, but non-standard permit implementations are historically risky. Validate whether name is immutable post-construction (it is set in constructor) and ensure no reinitialization paths exist. Also validate ecrecover malleability constraints (s/v) and whether integrators assume standard DOMAIN_SEPARATOR behavior.
deployment_assumptions: Integrations rely on EIP-2612 exact semantics and cache DOMAIN_SEPARATOR off-chain
Any unusual chain environment (forks/L2s) where chainId behavior differs, or any future upgrade/migration that changes name

threats.title: Genesis deposits can retroactively earn past Gauge rewards due to updateReward(address(0)) in depositsForGenesis
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2.depositsForGenesis calls _depositsForGenesis which uses updateReward(address(0)) rather than updateReward(tokenOwner). threats.title: Tautological Check Allows Setting Internal Bribe to Zero Address, Breaking Fee Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe uses a tautological check `_int >= address(0)` which always passes. Setting the bribe to address(0) causes claimFees to revert, permanently breaking LP fee distribution for that gauge.
threats.investigation_rationale: A simple admin typo can set the internal bribe to the zero address. Because claimFees attempts to approve and call notifyRewardAmount on this address, it will revert, trapping all accumulated fees in the Pair until the admin fixes the address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_int >= address(0), 'ZA'); is always true

preconditions: Admin calls setInternalBribe(address(0))

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _int >= address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: claimFees reverts on zero address interaction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, purely accidental DoS

economic_questions: How quickly can the admin respond to fix this if it occurs?
validation_requirements: Set internal bribe to address(0) and verify claimFees reverts.

threats.title: PairFactory setStakingFees Has No Combined Fee Cap Allowing LP Fee Diversion
threats.hypothesis_description: setStakingFees allows stakingNFTFee up to 3000 (30%). Combined with MAX_REFERRAL_FEE (unbounded per INV-ACCESS-001), the combined deduction from LP fees can exceed 100% of the swap fee amount. In _update0/_update1: referralFee is deducted first, then stakingNFTFee is computed on the remainder. If both are set high (e.g., referral=7000, staking=3000), LPs receive only amount * 0.3 * 0.7 = 21% of the original fee. While each setter has its own cap, no combined cap prevents near-total LP fee diversion to referral and staking systems.
threats.investigation_rationale: Fee distribution in _update0/_update1 applies referral and staking deductions sequentially with no floor on LP portion. FeeManager can legally set parameters that leave LPs with near-zero yield, discouraging liquidity provision.
threats.config_evidence: PairFactory.sol: setStakingFees requires _newFee <= 3000; setReferralFee has no upper bound

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_newFee <= 3000) but no combined cap with MAX_REFERRAL_FEE; setReferralFee has no upper bound check

preconditions: FeeManager sets both referralFee and stakingNFTFee to high values
Compromised or malicious feeManager

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: referralFee = amount * MAX_REFERRAL_FEE / 10000 (unbounded); stakingFee = (amount - referral) * stakingNFTFee / 10000 (up to 30%); LP gets remainder
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set MAX_REFERRAL_FEE=9700, stakingNFTFee=3000 → LP receives ~2.1% of fee amount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Divert >97% of swap fees away from LPs to referral and staking systems controlled by feeManager

economic_questions: What is the current MAX_REFERRAL_FEE setting?
Who controls the feeManager role and is it timelocked?
What is the maximum combined diversion possible?
validation_requirements: Calculate LP share for various fee parameter combinations
Verify underflow behavior when referralFee > amount
Check if any on-chain safeguards limit combined fee extraction

threats.title: PairFactory setGenesisStatus Missing isPair Validation Allows Genesis Flag on Arbitrary Addresses
threats.hypothesis_description: setGenesisStatus allows genesisManager or feeManager to set isGenesis[_pair] = true for ANY address, unlike setIsGenesis which validates isPair[_pairAddress]. RouterV2's getAmountOut skips pairs where isGenesis returns true, and addLiquidity blocks initial deposits to genesis pairs. Setting isGenesis on a legitimate non-genesis pair would block its swap routing and initial liquidity, effectively DoSing that pair's functionality.
threats.investigation_rationale: The missing isPair validation in setGenesisStatus creates an asymmetry with setIsGenesis. A compromised or careless genesisManager could mark active pairs as genesis, disrupting routing and liquidity operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setGenesisStatus(address _pair, bool status) — no require(isPair[_pair]) unlike setIsGenesis which has require(isPair[_pairAddress])

preconditions: Attacker has genesisManager or feeManager role
Target pair is actively used for trading

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set genesis status on active pair to disable its routing in RouterV2.getAmountOut

threats.title: GaugeExtraRewarder recoverERC20 Can Reduce Reward Rate Mid-Distribution With Imprecise Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: recoverERC20 allows the owner to withdraw reward tokens during active distribution. It recalculates rewardPerSecond as (notDistributed - amount) / timeleft. However, updatePool() is called first, which may leave some rewards accrued but unclaimed. The notDistributed = rewardPerSecond * timeleft may not account for rewards already accrued in accRewardPerShare but not yet claimed. Recovering tokens reduces the balance, and the new rewardPerSecond is computed on the reduced notDistributed, but existing user.rewardDebt values were set based on the old accRewardPerShare, creating a potential shortfall.
threats.investigation_rationale: The interaction between updatePool, pending claims, and the recover operation creates a window where the accounting for remaining rewards vs. what's actually claimable diverges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updatePool(); notDistributed = rewardPerSecond * timeleft; rewardPerSecond = (notDistributed - amount) / timeleft — doesn't account for accrued-but-unclaimed amounts in accRewardPerShare

preconditions: Owner calls recoverERC20 during active reward distribution
Users have unclaimed accrued rewards (accRewardPerShare * amount > rewardDebt)
Recovery amount is close to notDistributed

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: notDistributed = rewardPerSecond * timeleft represents future scheduled rewards, but doesn't subtract already-accrued-in-pool amounts
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner recovers tokens, new rewardPerSecond is too low to cover both accrued and future rewards
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Late claimers of extra rewards receive less than earned

economic_questions: How much can the shortfall be after recoverERC20?
Does updatePool() before recovery fully reconcile the state?
What is the maximum recovery that maintains solvency?

threats.title: PairFactory referral fee misconfiguration can permanently DoS swaps via underflow in Pair fee update
threats.hypothesis_description: PairFactory.setReferralFee and setCustomReferralFee have no bounds enforcement. If MAX_REFERRAL_FEE or customReferralFees[pair] is set above 10000, Pair._update0/_update1 computes referralFee = amount * maxRef / 10000 which becomes > amount, then executes `amount -= _referralFee`, underflowing and reverting every swap that tries to accrue fees. This halts trading protocol-wide for affected pairs (potentially all pairs if MAX_REFERRAL_FEE is used).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct invariant violation (INV-ACCESS-001). While exploitation requires privileged feeManager control (thus LOW realism under the bright-line rule), it is operationally critical because it can be triggered accidentally during routine parameter changes or through governance/role mistakes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(_refFee <= 10000); Pair._update0/_update1 does `amount -= _referralFee` where _referralFee = amount * maxRef / 10000

preconditions: feeManager (or genesisManager via setGenesisStatus path is irrelevant here) sets referral fee > 10000 globally or for a specific pair
Swaps occur on impacted pairs (fee accrual path executed)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fee parameter setter lacks basic bounds; Pair fee accrual uses subtraction that will underflow
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time fee parameters are updated

operational_questions: What governance/process controls exist around fee changes (review, simulation, staging)?
Are there automated parameter sanity checks in deployment/ops tooling?
Can customReferralFees be set on per-pair basis widely and accidentally brick specific markets?
validation_requirements: Unit test setReferralFee(10001) then perform Pair.swap and confirm revert
Check current production values for MAX_REFERRAL_FEE/customReferralFees
Review ops scripts for parameter setting and whether they validate bounds

threats.title: Gauge genesis deposits can retroactively steal past rewards due to missing per-user reward checkpointing
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2.depositsForGenesis updates global reward state with updateReward(address(0)) but does not checkpoint the beneficiary’s userRewardPerTokenPaid. If rewards were already accruing, a genesis depositor can later call getReward() and receive rewards as if they had been staked since the beginning, draining rewards allocated to earlier stakers.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct invariant violation (INV-STATE-001). Although only genesisPool can call depositsForGenesis (so attacker must be a genesis participant and the genesis system must trigger deposits after rewards started), this is a realistic operational sequencing bug during launches/epochs and can cause immediate reward insolvency or unfair distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _depositsForGenesis uses updateReward(address(0)) and never sets userRewardPerTokenPaid[tokenOwner] to rewardPerTokenStored

preconditions: Gauge has non-zero rewardPerTokenStored from prior distribution periods (notifyRewardAmount called earlier)
GenesisPool calls depositsForGenesis for a user after rewards started accruing
User later calls getReward() (public) or distribution calls getReward(user)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Genesis stake accounting is split across gauge and genesisPool; per-user reward checkpointing omitted
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires genesisPool action sequencing; not strictly admin-only once genesis runs
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever genesis deposits occur after any rewards accrual

operational_questions: Can notifyRewardAmount be called before genesis deposits complete in production workflows?
Does genesis launch pipeline guarantee deposits happen before any emissions begin?
Is genesisPool trusted to call depositsForGenesis only in a safe window?
